coredata.dc:title: Development of a rapid detection method for real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR of Salmonella spp. and Salmonella Enteritidis in ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables
coredata.dc:creator: Wan, Jiajia
Zheng, Liping
Kong, Liangyu
Lu, Zhaoxin
Tao, Yang
Feng, Zhiyang
Lv, Fengxia
Meng, Fanqiang
Bie, Xiaomei
coredata.dc:description: Two real-time quantitative PCR detection systems that contained DNA Green saturated fluorescent dye for detection of Salmonella Enteritidis and Ly Green saturated fluorescent dye for detection of Salmonella spp. were constructed. Furthermore, the specificity of the amplification system increased with the addition of graphene quantum dots. The R2 values of the standard curves were 0.999 (S. Enteritidis) and 0.998 (Salmonella spp.), respectively. The sensitivities of the two amplification systems were evaluated based on three aspects: genomic DNA (1.36 fg/μL, 1.36 fg/μL); bacterial cell without enrichment (2.49 CFU/mL, 2.49 CFU/mL); plasmid containing Gene ID:1828390769 (11.6 copies/μL) and plasmid containing Gene ID:3335471 (1.08 copies/μL). Detection results of fruits and vegetables artificially contaminated with Salmonella showed that the traditional culture method exhibits a lower detection rate than the molecular detection method, and the culture time of the Selective Luria-Bertani broth (SLB) was short. The detection rate was increased compared with that of the Buffered Peptone Water-Selenite Cystine broth (BPW-SC). The two detection systems exhibit high sensitivity and specificity and are suitable for detecting Salmonella in ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Salmonella
Real-time quantitative PCR
Rapid detection method
Saturated fluorescent dye

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Effects of five fluorescent dyes on S. Enteritidis (A–B) and Salmonella spp. (C–D) real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR.
Effects of GQDs on real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR. (A) Ct values of amplification. (B) Tm values of amplification.
Effect of GQDs on specific of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR.(A) Ct value of amplification result. (B) Melting curves.

Standard curve of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR for S. Enteritidis (A, C) and Salmonella spp. (B, D).A, C: 1–6 Plasmid concentration was 409 pg/μL-4.09 fg/μL; B, D: 1–6 Plasmid concentration was 38 pg/μL-0.38 fg/μL.
Experimental strain and specificity of real-time fluorescence quantitative PCR.

head.ce:title: Development of a rapid detection method for real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR of Salmonella spp. and Salmonella Enteritidis in ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing, 210095, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Nanjing Agricultural University, Nanjing, 210095, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 1 Weigang, Xuanwu District, Nanjing, Jiangsu, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two real-time quantitative PCR detection systems that contained DNA Green saturated fluorescent dye for detection of Salmonella Enteritidis and Ly Green saturated fluorescent dye for detection of Salmonella spp. were constructed. Furthermore, the specificity of the amplification system increased with the addition of graphene quantum dots. The R2 values of the standard curves were 0.999 (S. Enteritidis) and 0.998 (Salmonella spp.), respectively. The sensitivities of the two amplification systems were evaluated based on three aspects: genomic DNA (1.36 fg/μL, 1.36 fg/μL); bacterial cell without enrichment (2.49 CFU/mL, 2.49 CFU/mL); plasmid containing Gene ID:1828390769 (11.6 copies/μL) and plasmid containing Gene ID:3335471 (1.08 copies/μL). Detection results of fruits and vegetables artificially contaminated with Salmonella showed that the traditional culture method exhibits a lower detection rate than the molecular detection method, and the culture time of the Selective Luria-Bertani broth (SLB) was short. The detection rate was increased compared with that of the Buffered Peptone Water-Selenite Cystine broth (BPW-SC). The two detection systems exhibit high sensitivity and specificity and are suitable for detecting Salmonella in ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The Salmonella genus is divided into two main species: S. bongori and S. enterica (subspecies I), while the S. enterica subsp. enterica is responsible for 99% of salmonellosis cases in humans (El-Tayeb, Ibrahim, Al-Salamah, Almaary, & Elbadawi, 2017). Symptoms of food poisoning attributed to Salmonella are variable, including diarrhea, fever, stomach ache, vomiting, nausea, muscular or articular pain, and loss of appetite. For the infants, elderly and individuals with weakened immune systems, due to the presence of toxins in blood even to life-threatening (Bai et al., 2018; Chlebicz & Slizewska, 2018). The infection mechanism of Salmonella involves toxins produced by virulence islands (SPIs) that interact with the host organism (Ingram, Brodsky, & Balachandran, 2016).

Non-typhoidal Salmonella serotypes, including S. Typhimurium and S. Enteritidis, are food-borne pathogens, and important causes of bacterial enteric disease in both humans and livestock (Miller et al., 2014; Wiedemann, Virlogeux-Payant, Chaussé, Schikora, & Velge, 2015). Chicken and eggs are the main Salmonella vectors of human infection. It is estimated that 10–80% of retail raw chicken meat is contaminated with Salmonella, and these levels are increased in the summer (Samad, Abbas, Tanveer, Ahmad, & Mustafa, 2018). However, the frequency of foodborne diseases related to fruits and vegetables is increasing yearly (Wan et al., 2020). For instance, an outbreak of Salmonella infections sickened 68 people in nine America states, and epidemiologic evidence indicates that bagged peaches sold at Albrecht-Discount (ALDI) grocery stores were the likely source (CDC, 2020a). Since the last update on August 7, 2020, a total of 869 individuals who became sick after consuming onions have been reported from 47 states in the US (CDC, 2020b). On March 10, 2020, the Canadian Food Inspection Agency issued a recall notice for fresh bean sprouts that may be contaminated with Salmonella (Foodmate, 2020). The original protective mechanism is removed from fresh cut fruits and vegetables, which facilitates microbial invasion, thus affecting human health (Liang, Min, Bhandari, & Gao, 2017).

Considering the limited shelf life of fruits and vegetables and low levels of bacteria in samples, rapid accurate quantitative and more efficient methods of detecting Salmonella to improve the tracking and identification technology of pathogenic microorganisms are required (Vichaibun & Kanchanaphum, 2020). However, qualitative detection of pathogenic bacteria in food can no longer meet the current requirements. Real-time PCR can quantitatively detect microorganisms in a high-throughput fashion and exhibits high specificity and sensitivity as it is able to detect even a single bacterium or a single copy of target sequence of DNA (Bhagwat, 2004; Elsayed, Abdeeen, Akiela, Farouk, & Zahran, 2014). The characteristics of repeatability reduce the liability of false–positive results as well as lower time required for performing the technique has been favored by many laboratories (Meira, Costa, Villa, Ramos, & Mafra, 2016). The reporter molecule of real-time PCR may either be a simple fluorogenic DNA-binding dye or a fluorogenically labeled oligonucleotide (Mao, Leung, & Xin, 2007), including the widely used hydrolyzed TaqMan probes (Ahamad, Hossain, Uddin, Sultana, & Ali, 2019; Rong et al., 2018). However, probes are usually expensive, time-consuming to synthesize and are associated with high potential false-negative rates attributed to sequence variability within probe-binding sites, these features have prevented wider applications of the technique (Stephen Andrew Bustin, 2002). As an unsaturated DNA-binding dye, SYBR Green I is by far the best performing dye and consequently has becomes the most widely used qPCR dye; however, one of the problems associated with the dye is its high tendency to inhibit PCR and promote nonspecific amplification (Mao et al., 2007; Rubio, Penha F', Almeida, Barbosa, & Jr, 2019). Thus, this dye is not suitable for the quantitative detection of Salmonella in ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables.

Saturating DNA dyes, such as LC Green/LC Green Plus (Herrmann, Durtschi, Bromley, Wittwer, & Voelkerding, 2006; Wittwer, Reed, Gundry, Vandersteen, & Pryor, 2003), Eva Green (Hasanpour & Najafi, 2017) and SYTO family (Radvanszky, Surovy, Nagyova, & Kádasi, 2015; Singh & Mustapha, 2014), can bind to all minor groove positions of double-stranded DNA in a saturating manner. These dyes have been widely used in high-resolution melting (HRM) analysis due to their high sensitivity, stability, reliability and compatibility (Li et al., 2010). Due to less inhibition for PCR amplification, less likely to cause nonspecific amplification and generate strong fluorescent signals makes them more suitable for quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (PCR) compared to the SYBR Green I (Pawar et al., 2017).

In addition, QDs (quantum dots) could improve the specificity and sensitivity of PCR (Ma et al., 2009; Sang, Yang, Yuan, Ren, & Zhang, 2015). Sang et al. (2013) thought the polymerase activity could be partially blocked by QDs at the pre-PCR temperatures, resulting in the improvement of PCR specificity. Furthermore, non-specific amplification could be accumulated during repeated amplifications, which make aborted of multi-round PCR. Sang, Zhang, Yuan, and Liu (2018) found that PCR specificity could be retained even in the ninth-round amplification in the presence of QDs. Sang et al. (2015) demonstrated that a cost-effective high-throughput real time PCR based on QD triggered PCR could be established with high sensitivity and accuracy. While CdTe QDs used in those articles is generally toxic and very harmful to the environment. Thus, GQDs (graphene quantum dots) as nonbiological toxic nanomaterials was used in real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR in this experiment.

Based on the specific targets of Salmonella spp. (Gene ID: 3335471) and S. Enteritidis (Gene ID: 1828390769), this experiment aimed to build two real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR detection systems to meet the testing requirements for ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables. The influence of the addition of saturating DNA dyes and graphene quantum dots on real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR amplification was assessed. Additionally, the analytical parameters of standard curves for mature amplification systems, sensitivity, specificity and practical application verification of this method was appraised at the research level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Bacterial strains and DNA template
ce:section.ce:para: A panel of 39 bacterial strains was used to develop the assays (Table 1 ). The bacterial stock culture was stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments and DNA extraction. DNA template was extracted using a bacterial DNA kit (Omega Bio-Tek, Norcross, GA, USA) for all the experiments, while the boiling method was mainly used for DNA extraction for the experiment of section 2.8 and 2.9 (Wei, Zhong, Hu, & Zhao, 2018). All experiments were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Primer preparation and real-time PCR assay
ce:section.ce:para: Highly specific primers (Table 2 ) were designed based on the target sequences of Salmonella spp. (Gene ID: 3355471) (Kong et al., 2013), S. Enteritidis (Gene ID: 1828390769) (mined by our laboratory) and invA genes, and were synthesized by Gen Script Biotech Corp. (Nanjing, China). The real-time PCR assay was optimized based on a 20-μL reaction volume containing 2 μL 10 × PCR Buffer, 0.15 mM dNTP mixture, 0.20 μM of each forward and reverse primer, 0.025 U/μL Ex Taq HS enzyme (TaKaRa Bio INC. Dalian, China), 0.5X of SYBR Green I (Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd, Beijing, China), and 2 μL of genomic DNA. The final volume was adjusted with deionized water. The amplification reactions were performed using a step one plus Real-Time PCR system (Applied Biosystems, America) with an initial denaturation at 95 °C for 5 min, followed by 40 cycles of amplification at 95 °C for 15 s and 62 °C for 60 s. Melting curves were acquired using the following procedure: 95 °C for 15 s, 60 °C for 60 s and 95 °C for 15 s.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
Effect of different fluorescent dyes on the amplification systems
ce:section.ce:para: To screen the most suitable dye for real-time PCR, SYBR Green I was chosen for comparison with four saturating dyes: Eva Green (Biotium Inc., USA), Ly Green (Life ilab Biological Technology Co., Ltd, Shanghai, China), LC Green plus (Idaho Technology Inc., Salt Lake City, USA), and DNA Green (Tianenze Gene Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). Different amounts of five types of fluorescent dyes are dispersed to final concentrations of 0.5X, 1.0X, 1.5X, and 2.0X into the amplification system. When the concentration of the dyes is changed, care is taken to ensure that the concentration of other components in the system remains unchanged. Accordingly, the amplification procedure is performed as described above. Loading operations was performed in a biological safety cabinet (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQDs on the real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
Effect of different concentrations of GQDs on the amplification systems
ce:section.ce:para: 1 mg/mL GQDs (J&K Scientific Ltd., Beijing, China) at final concentrations of 0, 0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, and 0.5 mg/mL were added to the quantitative real-time PCR system, including 0.5X Ly Green in Salmonella spp. and S. Enteritidis with 1.0X DNA Green, respectively. Reactions were performed in a total volume of 20 μL with 2 μL of DNA template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of GQDs on the specificity of amplification systems
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis by the melting curve illustrated that some saturating dyes were mainly from the non-specific amplification products. It is very important to know whether GQDs can largely influence the specificity of target DNA products. GQDs (0.05 mg/mL) were added to the real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR system to perform amplification reaction, and the specificity of Eva Green and DNA Green dyes on the amplification of the Salmonella system were investigated; SYBR Green I and Ly Green exhibited specific effects on system amplification of S. Enteritidis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve preparation
ce:section.ce:para:
TA cloning method was used to prepare the recombinant plasmid. The method was performed as follows: i To obtain plentiful target genes, the primer of Salmonella spp. SC962 and S. Enteritidis SEP (Table 2) were used for high-fidelity common PCR amplification of S. Enteritidis genome DNA, separately. The PCR reaction components (50 μL) consist of 0.4 μM of each forward and reverse primer, 25 μL of 2X Pfu enzyme mix (Dongsheng Biotech Co., Ltd., Guangdong China), 5 μL of DNA template. The PCR amplification reactions were performed with 95 °C for 3 min; 35 cycles of 95 °C for 15 s, 62 °C for 15 s, and 72 °C for 40 s; and 72 °C for 5 min ii PCR products were purified and recovered using PCR purification columns (Qiagen, Valencia, CA), and blended with 5 U/μL Taq enzyme mix as 1:1 and incubated at 72 °C for 30 min. The products were mixed with pMD19-T vector (TaKaRa Bio INC. Dalian, China) before overnight at 16 °C to obtain recombinant plasmid. iii 10 μL of recombinant DNA was transferred into 50 μL E. coli DH5α competent cell, then spreading them on Luria-Bertani (LB) solid medium plates supplemented with 50 mg/mL Ampicillin and incubating at 37 °C. Colonies should appear after 48 h. Inoculate 10 mL of LB medium containing 50 mg/mL Ampicillin with several E. coli DH5α colonies from the same plate and grow for overnight. Plasmid DNA was extracted and measured the concentration using NanoDrop 2000 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA). The plasmid copy number was calculated according to formula (4.1) (Kong et al., 2013), and both plasmids were adjusted to 1 × 108 copies/μL. Typically, six gradient concentrations of plasmids with ten-fold serial dilutions were selected to construct the corresponding fluorescent quantitative standard curves.
660) Copy number/μL = [(6.02 × 1023) × (DNA concentration (ng/μL) × 10−9)]/(number of bases ×

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
ce:section.ce:para: The sensitivity of the real-time quantitative PCR system of Salmonella spp. and S. Enteritidis was investigated based on the following three aspects: i. DNA (initial concentration of 136 ng/μL) was diluted 10 times to evaluate the sensitivity of the DNA template; ⅱ. Ten-fold serial dilutions of the initial cultures (2.49 × 109 CFU/mL cells) were to extract the bacterial genome DNA for verifying the colony sensitivity; ⅲ. Plasmids containing S. Enteritidis Gene ID: 1828390769 (1.16 × 109 copies/μL) and Salmonella spp. Gene ID: 3345471 (1.08 × 109 copies/μL) were constructed, respectively. A 10-fold gradient dilution of each plasmid was used to determine the sensitivity of the amplification system to the plasmid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility evaluation of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether the amplification of the reaction system is stable in the case of low concentration templates. Genomic DNA of S. Enteritidis was extracted and diluted 10 times with physiological saline. The DNA concentration of 1.36 pg/μL and 1.36 fg/μL was chose, each concentration (2 μL) in quintuplicate was assessed in real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR. In addition, plasmids containing Gene ID: 1828390769 and Gene ID: 3355471 target fragments were also prepared and diluted to 1.16 × 104, 1.16 × 101, and 1.08 × 103, 1.08 × 100 copies/μL for reproducibility evaluation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
ce:section.ce:para: DNA purified from 19 Salmonella and 20 non-Salmonella strains (listed in Table 1) were used as a template to determine the specificity of fluorescent quantitative PCR of Salmonella spp. and S. Enteritidis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial contamination with salmonella
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh ready-to-eat fruits and vegetables, such as cabbage, lettuce, cucumber, carrot, tomato, apple, watermelon, melon, grape, and strawberry were obtained from Supermarket (Suguo, Nanjing, China). The samples were tested according to GB/T 4789.4–2013 (China standard) to ensure the absence of Salmonella contamination. Take six samples of each fruit and vegetable, each sample has a mass of approximately 25 ± 0.2 g. Three samples contaminated by 1 mL of 6.5 × 101 CFU/mL S. Enteritidis were soaked in 225 mL Selective Luria-Bertani broth (SLB) for enrichment, and other three samples infected by S. Enteritidis in 225 mL Buffered Peptone Water (BPW). After cultured at 37 °C for 10 h, 1 mL culture in SLB medium was obtained to extract DNA by the boiling method. Simultaneously, the culture in SLB medium was streaked on Xylose Lysine Desoxycholate Agar (XLD) and Bismuth Sulfite Agar (BS) selection medium to observe the presence or absence of Salmonella characteristic colonies. While 1 mL BPW culture was inoculated into 10 mL Selenite Cystine Broth (SC). After cultured at 37 °C for 24 h, 1 mL SC culture was taken out to extract DNA by the boiling method, and the culture was streaked on XLD and BS as well as. Extracted genomic DNA was assessed by real-time quantitative PCR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salmonella detection in naturally lettuce samples
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 41 lettuce samples were obtained from the Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences (Nanjing, Jiangsu, China), each sample is approximately 100 g. The lettuce was aseptically cut into pieces in a biological safety cabinet (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, USA), approximately 25 ± 1 g lettuce pieces were weighed as a sample. Each sample was separately put into 225 mL SLB and 225 mL BPW medium in duplicate, homogenized for 1 min, and cultured at 36 °C ± 1 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, 1 mL BPW culture was transferred into 10 mL Tetrathionate Broth Base (TTB), and incubated at 42 °C ± 1 °C for 24 h. Meanwhile, additional 1 mL BPW culture was inoculated into 10 mL SC, and incubated at 36 °C ± 1 °C. The enrichment solutions of TTB, SC and SLB were subject to the boiling method to extract DNA. The detection of Salmonella mainly adopts four methods: biochemical identification kit, LAMP, real-time quantitative PCR of Salmonella spp. and real-time quantitative PCR of S. Enteritidis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results
Establishment of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
Effect of different fluorescent dyes on amplification systems
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of five dyes at different concentrations on the Ct values of the S. Enteritidis amplification system was presented in Fig. 1 A. The Ct values were showed first increase after decline in as the dye concentration increases, and attained minimum when added 1.0X final concentration of five dyes in amplification system. The smallest Ct values were obtained in the presence of different concentration DNA Green comparing with Eva Green, Ly Green, LC Green plus and SYBR Green I. The Tm values of the S. Enteritidis amplification system with adding five dyes were increased as the concentration increases (Fig. 1B). There was evident enhancement of the Tm values when LC Green plus were added, with an increase of approximately 2 °C. In addition. Furthermore, we found that SYBR Green I and Ly Green were prone to produce nonspecific peaks (data no shown). To summarize what has been mentioned above, the final concentration of 1.0X DNA Green saturated dye yielded the optimal conditions for real-time quantitative PCR amplification of S. Enteritidis.

The effect of the five dyes on the Salmonella spp. amplification system was consistent, which were shown in Fig. 1C. The Ct values were reached the minimum at a 0.5X final concentration of five dyes, but the four gradient concentrations of Ly Green saturated dye were all less than other dyes. The 0.5X final concentration of Ly Green was the best chose for the real-time quantitative PCR system of Salmonella spp.. It should be noted that DNA Green was yielded the largest Ct values which was contrary to the result of the S. Enteritidis amplification system. With the addition of SYBR Green I, Ly Green and DNA Green dyes in the Salmonella spp. amplification system, the Tm values were slightly increased (Fig. 1D), and this finding was in contrast to the trend observed for Eva Green and LC Green plus, when the Ct values higher than 30 cycles, the Tm values decreased greatly. Additionally, the melting curve analysis proved that Eva Green and LC Green plus could largely generate nonspecific peaks (data no shown). It is noteworthy that Tm values of the Salmonella spp. amplification system with DNA Green was only approximately 84 °C, which differs from the Tm values of the other four dyes (88–91 °C). Overall, the effect of Eva Green and DNA Green on the real-time quantitative PCR system of Salmonella spp. were not as good as that of SYBR Green I, Ly Green and LC Green plus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of GQDs on the real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
Effect of different concentrations of GQDs on amplification systems
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the effect of GQDs on real-time quantitative PCR, a series of diluted GQDs was used. Fig. 2 showed the amplification data. It is clear that the Ct values of Salmonella spp. are lower than S. Enteritidis in our GQDs-faciliated qPCR system. The Ct values of Salmonella spp. amplification system with 0.025–0.2 mg/mL GQDs were decreased than the absence of GQDs (Fig. 2A). When 0.025–0.15 mg/mL GQDs were added in S. Enteritidis amplification system, there are having similar Ct values, providing little discrimination for detection of the GQDs concentrations. However, the maximum fluorescence values of amplification system with the above mentioned GQDs concentrations are higher than that without GQDs (data no shown). With the increase of GQDs concentration (>0.3 mg/mL), inhibitory effects were observed and the evident delay of Ct values was attained, even completely inhibit the amplification of Salmonella spp. and S. Enteritidis amplification system. Furthermore, the Tm values analysis demonstrated that the presence of GQDs made the Tm values in decline, which both in the S. Enteritidis and Salmonella spp. amplification systems (Fig. 2B). It was ultimately determined that 0.05 mg/mL GQDs should be added in the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of GQDs on the specificity of amplification systems
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 A, the Ct values of S. Enteritidis real-time quantitative PCR system using SYBR Green I or Ly Green as the fluorescent dye was less than that of control without GQDs. Even though 2.5X SYBR Green I and Ly Green were added in the amplification system, the Ct values still remained low. Furthermore, the melting curve results further demonstrated that the presence of 0.05 mg/mL GQDs was very efficient in eliminating non-specific amplification (Fig. 3B). These findings indicated that GQDs reduced the inhibitory effect of DNA dyes on the amplification, which broadened the use range of dye concentration.

In the real-time quantitative PCR detection for Salmonella spp. using Eva Green or DNA Green as fluorescence indicator, the Ct values with GQDs were higher than that of the control without GQDs, especially for DNA Green, which had no Ct values as the DNA Green concentration exceed 0.5X (Fig. 3A). However, we still could find that the presence of GQDs apparently resulted in much more specific amplification of the target products in the light of melting curve analysis (Fig. 3B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard curve preparation
ce:section.ce:para: Good standard curves with a high correlation coefficient 0.999 for S. Enteritidis detection system and 0.998 for Salmonella spp. detection system were calculated from the Ct values and dilution of DNA, respectively (Fig. 4 A–B). In addition, the amplification curves of Salmonella spp. detection system exhibited a greater inclination, that is to say the amplification efficiency was higher (Fig. 4D). However, the maximum fluorescence values of S. Enteritidis detection system was ten times as large as than that Salmonella spp., and a stronger fluorescence signal was noted (Fig. 4C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
Reproducibility evaluation of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
ce:section.ce:para: The Ct values were analyzed and compared between each concentration repeat according to the experimental results to verify the repeatability of the system amplification. S. Enteritidis and Salmonella spp. detection system both could detect the set templates of different concentrations (Table 6 ). While the Ct values were increased as the genomic DNA and plasmid concentration decreased, which in accordance with expectation. Noteworthiness, the standard deviation of the Ct value was less than 0.5, indicating the detection system exhibited good repeatability and stability for identification of the target gene, especially with using low amounts of template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR
ce:section.ce:para: The result of the specificity for two detection systems was shown in Table 1. For S. Enteritidis detection method, only S. Enteritidis CICC 21482 exhibited positive amplification. Other Salmonella and non-Salmonella samples did not exhibit amplification signal. Regarding the Salmonella spp. detection system, only 19 strains of Salmonella produced positive results, whereas the other strains were not detected using the real-time quantitative PCR system. From this, it could be seen that the two real-time quantitative PCR detection methods have promising specificity for 19 Salmonella strains and 20 non-Salmonella strains.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Artificial contamination with salmonella
ce:section.ce:para: Regardless of the use of SLB or BPW-SC medium, 6.5 × 101 CFU/25 g S. Enteritidis in the artificially contaminated fruit and vegetable samples was detected by S. Enteritidis and Salmonella spp. detection systems, the detection rate was 100% (Table 7 ). However, the detection rate of typical colonies based on streak plate method was considerably reduced compared with molecular detection methods. Meanwhile, the detection rate of streak plate method of through SLB broth to enrichment the Salmonella cells was higher than the BPW-SC national standard method (63.3% and 50%, respectively). In addition, with regard to meets the needs of molecular detection, the same detection effect was obtained after only 10 h of enrichment in SLB compared with 34 h for BPW-SC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Salmonella detection in naturally lettuce samples
ce:section.ce:para: Lettuce samples were detected using the LAMP liquid kit and two real-time quantitative PCR methods after 10 h SLB enrichment. As shown in Table 8 . Two lettuce samples, JD53 (grown in Liuhe base of Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences) and JD73 (grown in No. 1 farm), were found to be contaminated with Salmonella from 41 lettuce samples. The results were consistent with those using the biochemical identification kit. Meanwhile, the Salmonella in JD53 and JD73 samples was detected by LAMP liquid kit and Salmonella spp. real-time quantitative PCR method after 34 h enrichment as described in the national standard method. Furthermore, Salmonella spp. was also detected in SC16 (supplied by Sam member supermarket) using the LAMP liquid kit and Salmonella spp. real-time quantitative PCR method. However, it was not detected using the biochemical identification kit. There are no S. Enteritidis was detected in such samples through S. Enteritidis real-time quantitative PCR methods, no matter what enrichment method was used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion
ce:section.ce:para: Experts from the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and the World Health Organization (WHO) note that, the incidence of foodborne diseases ranks in the forefront of the total incidence of all diseases, emerging as one of the most health-threatening problems in the world (Jacxsens, Uyttendaele, Luning, & Allende, 2017). The purpose of this study is to construct a real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR method for rapid and accurate detection of S. Enteritidis and Salmonella spp. in food samples.

Firstly, the effects of four saturated dyes and SYBR Green I on amplification were investigated. The results showed that SYBR Green I had a negative effect on the Ct values of the S. Enteritidis amplification system which inhibited the amplification of the PCR system, and the amplification effect was not as good as that of saturated fluorescent dyes when the final concentration was 2.0X. This finding was in accordance with consistent with Sang et al. (2013). Moreover, Eischeid (2011) compared the performances of 23 different DNA dyes in real time PCR. Eva Green and SYTO dyes were the most desirable fluorescent dyes that were characterized with lower Ct values, increased fluorescence intensity and reduced inhibition of PCR compared with SYBR Green I.

Different dyes also have different effects on the amplification system. DNA green produced a better effect than Eva Green, Ly Green, LC Green plus and SYBR Green I (lowest fluorescence value) dyes in S. Enteritidis amplification system. While the function of Ly Green in the Salmonella spp. amplification system was more prominent. Among these dyes, LC Green plus had a relatively large impact on the Tm value with an increase of approximately 2 °C. Moreover, the high cost of LC Green plus dyes limits the application of this technology, especially when processing a large number of samples. Thus, it is necessary to identify new low-cost dyes to replace them (Li et al., 2010). In addition, the data showed that the S. Enteritidis detection system with SYBR Green I and Ly Green was prone to producing nonspecific peaks. In contrast, when any concentration of Ly Green saturated dye was added to the Salmonella spp. detection system, the Ct values of the amplification were less than that of other dyes. Ly Green, a new type of double-stranded DNA-binding saturated fluorescent dye, produces a stronger signal than SYBR Green I. The Ct values of Eva Green and DNA Green were both greater than that of SYBR Green I, demonstrating evident inhibition. This phenomenon was in contrast to the effect of these two dyes on the S. Enteritidis real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR system. In addition, DNA Green, Eva Green and LC Green plus were prone to producing non-specific peaks. Relevant studies have demonstrated that LC Green plus is specifically designed for mutation detection using the melting curve method. Saturation does not have an inhibitory effect on PCR, and Eva Green is unlikely to cause non-specific amplification (Mao et al., 2007). In general, to the best of our knowledge, this is the first time that DNA Green and Ly Green have been applied to fluorescent quantitative PCR.

Sang et al. (2013) found that CdTe QDs was efficient in eliminating non-specific amplification of SYBR Green I or Eva Green real-time amplification, thereby increased the PCR amplification efficiency in real-time format with a wide dynamic range. Simultaneously, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), another safe and non-toxic quantum dots, improved the overall performance of PCR in terms of its yield, sensitivity, and specificity (Zhu et al., 2015). This study found that 0.025–0.15 mg/mL GQDs had a positive effect on the Salmonella spp. real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR method compared with S. Enteritidis detection method. Namely, Salmonella spp. detection method could generate more lower Ct value. Compared with the control without GQDs, the Tm had a gradually decreasing tendency. The possible reason is below: the addition of QDs with an appropriate concentration can increase the specificity and yield of PCR amplification, whereas the adsorption of numerous components at a higher concentration will inhibit PCR amplification and reduce the yield (Sang et al., 2013). Actually, the addition of GQDs had effectively improved the specificity of the Salmonella spp. detection system based on Eva Green or DNA Green and the S. Enteritidis detection system with added SYBR Green I and Ly Green. The result further shows that GQDs can broaden the useful range of dyes and relieve the inhibitory effect of certain dyes on PCR.

Good standard curves with high correlation coefficient 0.99 were attained. Moreover, the two systems exhibit high sensitivity, good repeatability and strong specificity for low-quantity template. Meanwhile, the two systems could detect artificially contaminated 6.5 × 101 CFU/25 g Salmonella in fruit and vegetable samples. Liu, Singh, and Mustapha (2018) applied a multiplex fluorescent quantitative PCR based on Eva Green to detect pathogenic bacteria in vegetables, only 103 CFU/g Salmonella in vegetables could be detected. According to a series of data in this experiment, we found that molecular detection method used in conjunction with SLB one-step selective culture method had a higher detection rate and merely needed spend less testing time. Which saved 24 h at least compared with the BPW-SC national standard two-step enrichment method, and also saves time compared with the 20 h required by the procedure described by Fabiani et al. (2017). The same effect have also been verified in actual sample testing. Both LAMP and two detection systems are applicable to actual sample detection, the detection rate of Salmonella was 2/41 using the LAMP liquid kit and Salmonella real-time PCR method. Different food substrates, incorrect experimental design and data analysis have led to inconsistent results from fluorescent quantitative PCR experiments, and the data are very suspicious (S. A Bustin et al., 2009). We believe that the real-time fluorescent quantitative PCR method developed in this study can be used for the standardization quantitative detection of Salmonella.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
ce:section.ce:para: Jiajia Wan: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Software, Visualization. Liping Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Liangyu Kong: Writing – review & editing, Validation. Zhaoxin Lu: Resources. Yang Tao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Zhiyang Feng: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fengxia Lv: Project administration. Fanqiang Meng: Formal analysis. Xiaomei Bie: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key R&D Program of China, Grant No. 2018YFC1602500, by Agricultural Independent Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province (Grant No. CX (18)3053), Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD).
